region_of_origin: Americas (Mesoamerica); widely adopted in North India and globally
food_subgroup: Kidney beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), dried raw, whole

ontology.foodex2_description: Kidney beans (Phaseolus vulgaris), red, dried raw, whole

serving.description: 50g dry (approximately 1 standard serving of dried beans)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of raw dried red kidney beans is approximately 50g (about 3 tablespoons), which expands to roughly 125–130g after soaking and cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Red Kidney Beans / Rajma — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F09020019; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (Beans, kidney, red, mature seeds, raw; NDB No. 16027)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F09020019 is an exact match for raw dried red kidney beans. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB No. 16027 (Beans, kidney, red, mature seeds, raw). All macros and micronutrients are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; boiled red kidney beans (GI approximately 24)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 24 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled red kidney beans. The input record is raw dried kidney beans; GI is measured on the cooked (boiled) form as raw dried beans are not consumed directly. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (24 × 30.65g per 50g dry serving) / 100 ≈ 7.4. This is consistent with the input record's stated glycemic_load of 7.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1406mg/100g dry) with very low sodium (12mg/100g), plus high magnesium (138mg) and calcium (143mg)
hypertension.guidance: Red kidney beans are highly suitable for hypertension management: their potassium-to-sodium ratio is among the highest of any common food, supporting vascular tone and blood pressure reduction; the high magnesium content further contributes to arterial relaxation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~24) and very high dietary fibre (24.9g/100g dry), including resistant starch with a documented second-meal effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: Red kidney beans are among the most beneficial foods for Type 2 Diabetes management: their GI of approximately 24 is one of the lowest of any food, their high soluble fibre and resistant starch content substantially blunts postprandial glucose response, and regular consumption is associated with improved long-term glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (24.9g/100g), very low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, high folate (394mcg DFE), and high potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red kidney beans support cardiovascular health through multiple mechanisms: their high soluble fibre reduces LDL cholesterol, their negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol content minimise atherogenic risk, and their high folate content supports homocysteine metabolism, reducing a key cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (8.2mg/100g), high folate (394mcg DFE), high magnesium, and anti-inflammatory plant protein profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red kidney beans are suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis: their high iron content addresses anaemia of chronic inflammation, their folate supports cell repair, and their plant protein and mineral profile provides anti-inflammatory nutritional support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red kidney beans (rajma; Phaseolus vulgaris) are large, deep red-coloured dried legumes native to Mesoamerica and now central to North Indian, Mexican, Caribbean, and global cuisines. In their raw dried form they are firm, with a mild earthy aroma and a dense, starchy interior. They must never be eaten raw or undercooked: they contain naturally occurring phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) lectins that cause severe gastrointestinal poisoning. Safe preparation requires overnight soaking (8–12 hours) followed by vigorous boiling for at least 10 minutes to destroy lectins — slow cookers operating at low temperatures are insufficient for lectin destruction. After safe cooking, kidney beans have one of the lowest glycemic index values of any food (~24), the highest dietary fibre content of common legumes (24.9g/100g dry), and an outstanding mineral profile including 1406mg potassium, 138mg magnesium, 143mg calcium, and 8.2mg iron per 100g dry weight. They provide 22.5g protein and 337 kcal per 100g dry. Their resistant starch produces a documented second-meal effect, further reducing postprandial glucose at subsequent meals. Nutritionally, they are among the most beneficial foods for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management. In North Indian cuisine they are the basis of rajma curry and rajma chawal (kidney beans with rice); in Mexican cuisine they are used in chilli con carne; in Caribbean cuisine in rice and peas. The small Kashmiri variety is particularly prized in North Indian cooking.
common_user_queries: how to cook kidney beans safely

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F09020019), an approved primary regional source, with cross-reference confirmation from USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 16027). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are exact matches. All values are internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for raw dried red kidney beans is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo Database (Food Code F09020019); USDA SR Legacy reports total fat of 1.06g/100g with monounsaturated 0.077g and polyunsaturated 0.528g — these values were not carried forward as the primary source (HPB) does not report them and the USDA values are for a slightly different moisture basis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for raw dried kidney beans is not reported in the HPB Foodo source used. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed 0.0 — kidney beans are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed 0.0 — kidney beans are a plant food and naturally contain no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed 0.0 per HPB source. GI of 24 is measured on the boiled form; raw dried beans are not consumed directly. GL per 50g dry serving calculated as (24 × 30.65) / 100 = 7.4. Lectin safety note: raw or undercooked kidney beans contain phytohaemagglutinin (PHA) and must be boiled vigorously for at least 10 minutes after soaking; slow cookers at low temperatures are insufficient.
